text: This video demonstrates the excision of a rectovaginal endometriotic nodule by a combined laparoscopic and vaginal approach. The patient is a thirty-seven years old woman, gravida one, para one. She had a previous cesarean section. She had severe pain in the right adnexia, radiating to the rectum at the time of menstruation. MRI demonstrated a three to four centimeter endometriotic nodule in the rectovaginal space. On speculum inspection, a four centimeter endometriotic nodule is apparent on the posterior fornix.

Laparoscopic view shows rectal involvement and obliteration of the cul-de-sac by endometriosis. The surgeon carefully grasps the peritoneum over the sacro-uterine ligament and opens it using an ultrasonic energy instrument. Dissection proceeds along the fatty tissue pathways between the rectum and utero-sacral ligament, advancing bilaterally in the perirectal space to the level of the levator plate.
The surgeon continues dissection in the left perirectal space, progressively liberalizing the rectovaginal space bilaterally below the adhesion point between the rectum and uterus.

The surgeon continues the dissection, fully separating the rectum from the uterus and freeing the cul-de-sac. The rectovaginal space is opened, revealing an endometriotic lesion on the rectal serosa, which is palpated with the tip of the aspirator.

The surgeon elevates the posterior vaginal fornix with an index finger placed in the vagina beneath the endometriotic nodule. A colpotomy incision is made over the index finger to enter the vagina. The nodule is located and palpated through the incision, with the ureter visibly close to the lesion site.
The surgeon excises the endometriotic nodule in the rectovaginal space using electrocautery via the vaginal route. The nodule is grasped with two forceps and pushed back into the pelvis.

The surgeon approximates the colpotomy incision using two ovicral sutures placed vaginally, while carefully monitoring the proximity of the suture bites to the ureter through laparoscopic visualization.

You can see the specimens excised in these photographs. In conclusion, rectovaginal endometriotic nodules can be excised vaginally after the dissection and liberalization of the rectovaginal space by laparoscopy, thus securing the bowel and ureter from inadvertent injury.
